dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious patient at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, I'm a paramedic with the rescue squad. What happened here?
I found him unconscious in his bedroom about 5-10 minutes ago.
He doesn't appear to be responding to verbal or physical stimuli.
No eye movement when I touched his shoulder or shouted his name.
No verbal response even when I pinched his fingers.
No motor movement recorded-he's in a full GCS 3 state.
Airway is patent and clear of obstruction-no sounds or blockage.
Spontaneous breathing, but it's shallow at about 12 breaths per minute.
Pulse is strong at 108 and blood pressure is 136/83 right now.
Pupils are 2 mm and not reacting in the beginning assessments.
Skin is warm, dry, and pink throughout with normal elasticity.
Rate is 12 and shallow, no signs of effort or strain.
Heartbeat is 108 beats per minute and regular in rhythm.
Blood pressure is 136 over 83-on the higher side of normal.
SpO2 measured at 95% with standard oxygen flow.
Glucose reading is at 0-this could indicate a metabolic or opioid cause.
Preparing to assist ventilations via BVM with 15 liters per minute oxygen.
Will insert a 28F nasopharyngeal airway in the right nostril to clear the airway.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia-fast rhythm but no arrhythmia detected.
Starting 4-lead ECG monitoring to track ongoing cardiac function.
Inserting 18-gauge IV in the right arm for quick medication delivery.
Administering 0.5 mg of Narcan intravenously. Patient starts showing responses.
Full head and neck exam shows normocephalic appearance with no deformities.
No external bleeding, bruising, or trauma in the head, ear, nose, or throat area.
Chest is even with no pain-breath sounds are clear bilaterally.
No signs of jugular venous distention above the sternal angle.
No tenderness or distension reported during initial examination.
No significant pain or instability noted in the pelvis during palpation.
Right arm had a recent track mark near the IV site; atraumatic on the other limbs.
Heart rate elevated to 125 bpm-Narcan response.
Breathing has progressed to 18 per minute but remains shallow.
Blood pressure rounds down to 0/0-systolic very low, diastolic flat.
Oxygen saturation remains at 95%, oxygen delivery is holding steady.
EKG still shows sinus tachycardia without new changes.
Most likely opioid overdose based on vitals and Narcan response.
He had been acting out of character earlier, very quiet despite usual social behavior.
Patient currently denies any known medications on board.
No drug allergies have been reported by the friend or patient to date.
No documented medical history or prior conditions.
Heart rate is 125, respirations up to 18, blood pressure is undetectable now.
Applying ETCO2 nasal cannula to monitor breath output continuously.
Pupils now 3 mm and reactive-neuro status improving steadily.
Follow-up history has not revealed any medical or drug use history.
Patient currently reports no pain when examined or questioned.
Patient is refusing transport, but stay in observation is being kept open.
Automatically monitoring via ETCO2 and EKG with no planned new actions yet.
Adhered strictly to protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose management.
Patient remains in place with final decision to decline transport AMA.
Responsiveness improved to AVPU A after Narcan with eye tracking and voice response.
Airway remains stable with no compromise or need for re-intubation.
Rechecking HEENT for trauma, noting no new injury or lesions.
No worsening of pain or distension, no acute abdominal issue detected.
Reassessing right arm vein shows IV secure and track mark still noted.
Vital signs remain at 125 bpm, 18 resp, flat diastolic, 95% O2.
Continuing passive observation with no immediate new procedures requested.
No newly disclosed medications, allergies, or history during reassessment.
Transport will not occur on patient's request as they remain alert and coherent.
Full progression of overload protocol executed to plan.
Companion signed AMA form; patient's case closed with full recovery.
Patient's full awareness and status confirmed before ending clinical care.
Patient designated to be under home care with ongoing instruction given.
All actions followed by protocol guidelines and consistent with outcome improvement.
Last allele met; documentation complete and transport decision accounted for.
